transcript.content: Lily Ray here and today we're going to talk a bit about the convergence of SEO and influencer marketing. Now, influencer marketing is obviously not a new trade in the marketing world. It's something that's been around for a while and definitely been something that people are focusing on all over the world. Uh it's taking the world by storm in certain parts of the world. It's just completely rampant with how people are focusing their marketing strategies. So, that's nothing new. Uh, but what I do want to talk about today is how SEO plays a role and where I think this is going in the future. So, I'm going to talk about Google specifically today and I want to talk about five clues that I think Google's giving us in terms of the direction that they want SEO to go in the future and how focusing on influencers and content creators and real experts is going to play a role going forward. So, the first clue is Google perspectives. This is a new filter that Google has been slowly rolling out this year to its organic search results. And for certain queries, you'll be able to see perspectives directly in the search results. This is something that they're still testing. It's not completely available for everyone around the world just yet. But essentially the way it works is that when you Google a question that would be best answered with various different perspectives from different people, Google might directly show you that perspectives filter in the search results which will highlight contributions from maybe Tik Tok, YouTube shorts, personal blogs, Reddit, Quora, places where people have actually contributed authentic answers and perspectives to questions. So as it stands, this is again still being rolled out. Some people see it uh as a filter that appears that you can click on and see a handful of different perspectives for different topics. Other people are seeing it directly in their search results. Some people are not seeing it at all, but it's absolutely something we should focus on and something that Google unveiled as a new product launch this year in Google IO. Number two is for me the most obvious one. It's the new E in Google's concept of EAT. So for a number of years we we've had EAT which stood for expertise, authoritiveness, and trustworthiness. In December of 2022, Google added a new E to this acronym, and that was added to its search quality raider guidelines. And this new E stands for experience. Now, if you had been paying attention to Google's communications leading up to that point, they talked a lot about the importance of firsthand experience, especially for things like product reviews where searchers are showing time and time again that they really want to hear from real human beings. They really want proof and evidence that the products have been tested by real human beings. And what that E is looking to do is to elevate content where firsthand experience really provides a better result. So things like product reviews. This is something that is in Google's quality raider guidelines. It's something that I believe Google is algorithmically still trying to figure out. Seems like a pretty big challenge. And this is why you won't necessarily always see perfection in terms of what currently ranks as far as firsthand experience goes. But this was obviously where Google wants the algorithm to go. Now, of course, influencers, social media marketers, people who have firsthand experience and perspectives on certain topics are going to do really well in this new uh this new situation. Number three is what Google has been doing for a number of years related to both authorship and the knowledge graph. Now, these are big topics in SEO. They're nothing new. They've been around for a long time. But if you pay attention to how Google is constantly evolving and innovating, how it shows certain authors and certain experts in the search results, what knowledge graphs and knowledge panels look like for individual experts. This is something that's always being refined. It's always becoming more robust. The knowledge graph is always expanding in information and Google's really trying to build out these online profiles for individual experts and authors who contribute to different publications. Now, it's very important to understand this is a nuanced topic. This is not to say that authorship is necessary for SEO performance or that you need to be in the knowledge graph to be considered an expert on Google. I don't believe those things are true. But I do believe there's a very strong relationship between people who are recognized on Google, particularly authors on your money or your life topics or maybe authors for major journalism publications and what Google knows about you. So if you have a robust knowledge graph, if you're an author and Google knows which books you published, what you specialize in, what is your background, I do believe this will be how Google evaluates EAT for many different topics going forward. So the more that you can take the individual experts, contributors, people with experience at your brand, at your company, elevate those people, build out profiles for them, make sure that search engines really understand who they are, the better I believe that your content will perform over time. Number four is the prevalence of short videos and YouTube directly in Google search results. Obviously, Google has an incentive to show YouTube for many different types of queries, but if you look right now at a large percentage of search results as well as your Google discover feed, you're going to see that YouTube gets to rank a lot. Video content ranks a lot. And of course, Google's going to show YouTube predominantly above other video providers. That's not to say that Tik Tok results don't also show up in search. Especially lately, we're seeing more and more Tik Toks in things like short videos, even in Google Discover, but the message is clear. Google loves video. Here I am doing a video right now because Google loves video. They're putting video in all different types of places. There's so many people that go directly to YouTube to get information. And Google's elevating this again across things like Google Discover. You'll almost always see a little carousel for short videos. And this is because Google knows that many users, particularly younger users, love consuming video content. In some cases, they're looking only at video content. They're not necessarily reading articles as much anymore. So, videos are the future. And how do you do good videos? You bring in people that know about the topic. You bring in influencers and you have them share their perspectives. This is a in large part the content that people want to see when they're going on places like YouTube. They want to hear from the experts. They want to see what influencers that they follow have to say about certain topics and believe about certain topics. This is something that I really believe is actually just like shaping our culture right now. So, it's absolutely worth brands paying attention to this trend. Number five is one that kind of slid uh under the radar, but Google was shown testing social media follower accounts directly in the search results in the past couple of weeks. I believe this was from Spain that someone uh posted this on Twitter. But essentially what it's doing is when you type an individual expert's name or an influencer's name or somebody that Google recognizes, it was actually showing their follower accounts from Twitter from or rather X from LinkedIn, from Instagram, from different places where they have followers and showing that follower count directly in the search results. Now whether or not this is something that will stick, something that I personally believe is a good idea, I don't. Um this is something that Google's testing. Why would they be interested in showing this data? Because they want to see how users respond to it. They want to see if it's something that's beneficial to searchers on Google. And if it turns out that the data shows that it is, we might be expecting in our future to see follower accounts directly on Google. Now, not saying it's a ranking factor, but if that's something that matters as far as how users engage with your content on Google, clickthrough rates, how much people trust the content that they're reading. Now, social media follower counts just became even more important for SEO. So again, all of this is indirect. Social media signals are not a direct ranking factor, but there's so many different clues that there's going to be this big intersection that's starting now between SEO, social media marketing, influencer marketing, and making sure that your brand is leveraging real people with real thoughts, real authentic beliefs and opinions and experience and elevating those people, not just in your written content, but in visual content, audio content, podcasts, and much, much more. Hope that was helpful. See you guys later.

author: Aleyda Solís
primary_role: Founder, Orainti – International and AI-informed SEO
focus: International SEO, AI-informed content strategy, and scalable SEO systems
source_notes: Listed among top AI search and SEO experts to follow in 2026.

transcript.content: What are the top tips to win SEO in 2026? I asked these questions to many of the SEOs who attended the Google Search Central event last week in Zurich and these are the answers. Let's go through them. >> My SEO tip for 2026 is to get yourself and your website and your business ready for the Agentic web. If you've been paying attention in 2025, you will see that we've seen the Aentic commerce protocol, the A2A protocol, the Agentics payment protocol, all of these things lining up during 2025. And in 2026, I think we're going to see all of those things really come to bear for the wider web. >> My SEO tip is to master your feeds and optimize all the data that we're passing directly into companies like Google and Open AI. So, our opportunity to give concrete facts and clear instruction to those systems and hopefully protect ourselves from generative results that could distort or wrongly reference us. So, that's what I say. Take control of your feeds. Find out which teams run them, which teams build them, and get them to start including your optimization efforts into 2026. invest heavily in proprietary data so that LLMs can use that data and then soak it up and then you know you can deny them access in the future um and then make them pay for it. Uh because the way the the web is going is that it's going to be more costly to build content and LLMs are going to run out of content to consume. So the more proprietary data you have, the higher the chance that you'll be able to broker deals with these these companies. >> So I have three SEO tips for 2026. Number one, if you're in e-commerce, your packaging is now your landing page because with machine vision, you better present that correctly. Customers are taking photos and asking questions. Tip number two, get familiar with clickstream data because if you're tracking prompts and trying to figure out your AI visibility, you have to confront your ideal prompts with reality and what people do. And that's with clickstream. Okay? And number three is get comfortable with the technical side of branding. handle that stuff that the branding department want won't like hallucination containment. What do you do when there's brand drift where features are made up? Who handles that? Who handles the bots and checking, hey, is this bug coming to train or answer a user question? That's on you. Now, >> my SEO tip is don't forget about internal linking. Optimize your internal linking and not just back links. My tip for next year is of course focus on indexation because in the future you might have some issues with it because of all that spammy content out there. Hi everyone. My SEO tip for next year is to focus on the tactics on what always worked. We have just heard it at Google uh search central like just do SEO keep doing SEO and you will be ranking in AI as well. And I really really hope to have a Google search console for AI overview. So please Google just do it. >> My SEO tip for 2026 is to actually bite the bullet and face your Google Analytics 4 or Adobe Analytics data head on. Organic is facing a lot of issues with lots of clicks and lots of sessions and the journey is becoming more multimodal. So you really want to be drilling down into advertising reports and looking at how much value and new to brand touch points organic is driving to your website and then which channels are then taking the preference through last click. It's G4 is not the greatest. We all dislike it, but it's time to actually start doing that and telling better data stories to stakeholders and to sea level. Hi guys, I'm here to tell you my SEO tip for 2026 is that SEO is going to be more exciting than it's ever been. We've got more services and more data to find out what users really want. So get smart, use it. Long live SEO. >> My tip for 2026, I think SEOs will start putting more emphasis on understanding their audience. So what I'll what I believe is going to happen is that there's going to be more uh attention paid to like media consumption. So if you think of who your audience are and you know who they are, where they live and what they do in a day. So for example, who you wake up, you have breakfast, what websites are you reading, what social media platforms are you going on, and then maybe you you get in your car or you go on the tube to the office, then what podcasts you listening to. So if you imagine that process throughout the day, I think more attention is going to be paid to who the audience is and where they're going to consume media and then they're going to factor that into how to reach those people through different different forms of content. and then optimize for that accordingly. And then that's going to factor into understanding this this messy middle which ultimately helps us with uh optimizing for things like AI overviews and large language models. So my top SEO tip for 2026 is and guess what it is? Schema. It's always been schema. But next year I think as LLMs get a bit more sophisticated with understanding what's under the hood in code, they'll want to save a bit on token optimization. They want to save those tokens and understand what an entity is and what the meaning of something is in as much less time as possible. And I think by doing that, that's where schema comes in. Tells you what the entity is, tells you what the context is, and then it can read the format and the structure of the content on it. To wrap up, I want to share my own SEO tip for 2026 and it is to every time that you do anything, create content, optimize content, enable a new section, take actions to improve the indexibility, crawlability, rendering of your content, anything is to ask if this is align with your brand. Is this on brand? Because realistically, we need to be much more mindful on what we invest our efforts on. It's not about stopping to invest on informational top of the funeral content. No, it's making sure that that informational top of the funeral content is actually meaningful to our brand. It's very well connected with our product offering or service offering. That's the key here. And it should be a question that should be asked not only when you create new content or optimize content for that matter, but whenever you do anything to deliver an experience to the user that should be aligned to the way that you want to be recognized. And this also can be very well extrapolated and not only on what you do on page but with the alignment of your marketing efforts elsewhere as well. Digital PR uh community management we can see how a lot of the answers from LLM's citations are coming from these external sources. So again, not only brand building, but brand optimization, entity optimization, alignment from a topical standpoint to create and optimize content in a way that is very well connected with your topical expertise should be a must and is my tip for next year. If you want to keep up with the latest in SEO, don't miss out and remember to subscribe to the SEO for newsletter. Last but not least, I want to thank Similar Web, who is an SEO for you to read and crawling Monday's sponsor, and who are also sponsoring this video. Thank you very much. Until the next one, and don't forget to subscribe to avoid missing

transcript.content: What are the predictions and expectations of the SEO community for 2026? This was the question that I asked to many SEOs who attended last week the Google search central event in Zurich and I got very very interesting including the ones of Joe Mueller and Barry Schwarz. Let's go through them. >> My top expectation for the 2026 year is that more publishers will continue to see negative declines in Google search. My prediction for 2026 is going back to the fundamentals and going back to your audience. One of the things that people forget is ultimately we're there for our customers and the more you know about your customers and their needs and their objective where LLMs actually are really handy in helping you do that, the better your results are going to be. So rather than chasing the shiny new thing that's coming along, you need to think about your audience and your fundamentals. >> My prediction for SEO in 2026 is avoid shiny toy syndrome. Focus on the SEO fundamentals. Keep an eye on AI, but depending on where you are as a business, focus on the SEO fundamentals. Don't get sucked into all the cool tools and pay a fortune. Focus on the SEO fundamentals and what will move the needle and achieve your goals for your business. So my prediction for search in 2026 is um attribution. It's going to change. A lot of the user journey is going to take place offsite in LLM and other AI platforms. So that's something I think brands need to think about and look for ways to get around that and be comfortable with less traffic coming to their sites potentially. My expectation on what is going to happen in SEO in 2026 and AI search is I think that people are going to continue using AI search more deeply and more frequently forformational queries only. I think that people will push themselves over to Amazon more frequently in order to do retail queries and I think Google will lose a little bit of volume but not much at all. I really think Google will stay the dominant player for finding things and with the links in the search results or sorry the AI results we will see more clicks from AI into search or AI results links and we will see that people actually come from those as well as from Google. So it will become an important channel but never as important as Google. I'm sorry. My SEO prediction for 2026 is that half of the AI trackers will die. >> My SEO prediction is that in 2026 the humans become more important than AI. As companies start realizing that AI has ruined a lot of marketing and they want to bring the humans back in who understand how to sell to other humans and the value of SEO and the value of SEO strategists will skyrocket. So I am excited to see this happen in 2026. I'm excited for the future of SEO and I'm excited to see how these brilliant marketers bring AI in as a tool instead of a solution. >> My SEO prediction for 2026 is that I think that content creators will be more important than uh today and we will use them and we will incorporate them in all the content strategies that we will work together. So my SEO expectation for 2026 would be that there will be more AI involvement in the search results both on the algorithmic side as well as on the UX side and that can be a challenge and an opportunity for especially for SEO people I guess because the challenge is to try to figure out what makes AI different from regular search results. results and what SEOs needs to do different in order to optimize for that. It's also a neat challenge because if you manage to keep up with everything, then you have the opportunity to stand out versus more general marketers that can't really be on top of SEO and everything else and might be left behind. >> So, my SEO prediction for 2026 is people will have to build more business cases. I think there's so many new things around. there's AI search, there's um people having difficulties to prioritize. And I think the more you actually try to build a real case behind it, the easier it is for you to get anything prioritized with your development teams. And so if you don't build these cases, you will end up just building a lot of things out there that have no impact. And by the end of the year, you will you will think you have done a lot of work, but ultimately there is there's no impact on the business. My SEO prediction for 2026 is that Google overviews and the zeroclick searches will dominate SERs. Keyword tracking tools will all die. >> So I would say that my SEO prediction also based on what I just saw is that schema markup and schema markup that really gives the user an insight on how shipping is going to happen is going to be quite important. And if you get that right, you have an competitive edge over other market participants. And I think implementing that and getting that whole line of shipping right is going to be quite important next year. >> When we talk about the prediction, I think that we should think about the past and look what really happened there and related to the SEO and AI searches. I believe that uh the most important thing is related to semantic about the entities about to recognize what really users search and of course the next big thing is how to combine the data related to GA4 GSC and other tools related to the world of AI and SEO. All right, my SEO prediction for next year is they're going to be some updates in Google search and some of them you will like and maybe some of them you won't but they're going to be updates and they will happen. Schema is actually going to be huge, huge, huge, huge again across everywhere next year or maybe next year and onwards because they've revealed here that there's going to be additional like use of schema on shopping, but it's the fact that schema adds structure in an unstructured mess, which is obviously LLMs and so forth. So start to reimplement schema as much as you possibly can. Mark up all the things. My SEO prediction for 2026 is that people are going to finally start looking at the actual traffic. So for years a lot of us in SEOs have wanted more traffic, more traffic or at least the clients have. But it wasn't really traffic that converted. transcript.content: How to successfully pitch and speak at SEO and digital marketing conferences. If you want to be a successful speaker, this is the Curly Mondays that you need to watch. On one hand, I have Kirsty Holkin, head of events and marketing at Web Certain. Hello Kirsty, how are you? Hi Ala, I'm great and I'm so happy to be here. Thanks so much for having me. >> Thank you very much for coming. On the other hand, I have Kelvin Newman who's the organizer, founder, director of two of the best known conferences in our sector, Brighton SEO at Herocom. Hello Calvin, how are you? >> Yeah, good, good. So yeah, also very happy to be here as well. So yeah, I'm happy to share my experience and hopefully yeah, give some people some helpful advice. I'm very looking forward to learn myself as well. So like I think sometimes people think because we've said no to them it's because their idea is bad or we didn't like it. And that's very much not the case. In fact, there's hundreds of you know applications that we've got that I would like to program. But there's only a very finite number of slots that we've got and you know like it's a kind of quite tricky challenge to balance what we're we're speaking and there's many moving parts in it. So the first thing is like if you rejected it didn't mean there was anything wrong with your talk in a lot of cases. It just could be you know we had lots of similar topics. We've had you speak recently. You spoke at other events. just on that particular moment in time, the way in which someone framed their pitch just appealed to me um in a quite a different way. So first of all, I would say, you know, you might have had a really good pitch, you might have had a really good idea, but then there's also I suppose it sometimes it's around either not being unique enough. So it might be really interesting but it might be quite similar to other ones we've pitched and you know we accepted someone six weeks ago who pitched a very similar sort of topic or the titles are sort of too vague. So they might be kind of quite a big topic and our format is we have lots of tracks running concurrently um so we want them to be narrow so there's different things for different people at different times and they're quite short at 20 minutes. So sometimes people almost kind of go, I'm going to do a talk about, you know, link building or digital PR and be very general and it's kind of like, well, I'd much rather something very very very specific if possible. >> Yeah, I think we experience a lot of the the same thing. I think for me, one of the mistakes I guess we see is just lack of awareness either about the event focus or about the audience.
